critic_raw:
The design spans two years of hospital discharge data and uses a stratified random split with the acknowledgment that 'moderate autocorrelation of patient readmissions over time' is expected but 'prioritizes detection of robust cross-sectional signals.' The practical implication is that hospital policy changes, new care protocols, and seasonal disease patterns will be equally distributed across train and test, meaning the model is evaluated under the same distribution it trained on. This is evaluating memorization of training-time patterns, not generalization to future patients — which is the clinical use case.

The RNN receives NLP-encoded discharge summaries alongside structured EHR features. However, NLP encoding of clinical text is a complex pipeline (tokenization, embedding, potentially a pretrained language model), and the design does not describe how this encoding is done or whether it is fitted exclusively on training data. If a pretrained language model is used, its training data may include clinical text that temporally overlaps with the test set, introducing leakage through the language model itself.

The design mentions 'cross-validation' for hyperparameter optimization alongside a stratified validation set, but does not specify whether cross-validation is applied to the full dataset or only the training set. If cross-validation folds are drawn from the full dataset before the test set is held out, this constitutes nested leakage: information from what should be the test set influences hyperparameter selection.

Department variability is controlled by comparing to 'department-focused models,' but this is described as a diagnostic comparison rather than a structural element of the experiment. If department-level models are not included in the final model selection and evaluation framework, this control is informational only and does not prevent department-level confounding from inflating the aggregate AUROC estimate.
all_issues_raised: The stratified random split ensures train and test are drawn from the same two-year distribution, so the model is evaluated on memorizing training-time patterns rather than generalizing to future patients — the actual clinical use case.

The NLP encoding pipeline for discharge summaries is unspecified — if a pretrained language model is used, its training corpus may temporally overlap with the test set, introducing leakage through the language model itself.

The design mentions both a validation set and cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning without clarifying whether cross-validation is applied to the full dataset or only the training partition, leaving open the possibility of nested leakage.

Department-level model comparisons are described as diagnostics rather than structural controls, so department-specific confounders can still inflate the aggregate AUROC without triggering any experimental safeguard.
all_issues_adjudicated: The stratified random split ensures train and test are drawn from the same two-year distribution, so the model is evaluated on memorizing training-time patterns rather than generalizing to future patients — the actual clinical use case.

The NLP encoding pipeline for discharge summaries is unspecified — if a pretrained language model is used, its training corpus may temporally overlap with the test set, introducing leakage through the language model itself.

The design mentions both a validation set and cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning without clarifying whether cross-validation is applied to the full dataset or only the training partition, leaving open the possibility of nested leakage.

Department-level model comparisons are described as diagnostics rather than structural controls, so department-specific confounders can still inflate the aggregate AUROC without triggering any experimental safeguard.
